Psychiatric Risk References for Threat Modeling

Clinical Outcome References
for Threat Modeling.

61 of 109 catalogued BCI attack techniques reference symptoms matching DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria — for threat modeling purposes only. These references are based on known neuroanatomical pathways and functional neuroscience, but neuroscience's understanding of brain function remains incomplete. These are not diagnostic claims. They require clinical validation by psychiatrists and neuroscientists before informing any clinical practice.

61

techniques with
psychiatric outcomes

29

DSM-5-TR
diagnoses linked

5

diagnostic
clusters

52

direct-risk
pathways

Threat Modeling Reference Only — Not Diagnostic Guidance

These DSM-5-TR references are used strictly for security threat modeling — illustrating the potential clinical severity of sustained BCI attack patterns. They are not diagnostic claims and have not been reviewed or validated by psychiatrists or clinical neuroscientists.

Neuromodest caveat (Morse, 2006): Neuroscience does not yet fully understand how the brain produces cognition, emotion, or consciousness. Neural correlates do not prove causation. The mappings here reflect current knowledge of neuroanatomical pathways and functional neuroscience, but this knowledge is incomplete and actively evolving. Any projection from signal-level disruption to psychiatric outcome carries inherent uncertainty that no framework can eliminate at this stage of the science.

This page exists as a living research artifact — a precursor to the validated clinical mappings that will become possible as neuroscience matures. Clinical validation, interrater reliability studies, and expert panel review are required before these references can inform clinical decision-making. See Section 9 of the preprint for the full limitations disclosure.

For Clinicians

A patient presents with sudden-onset anxiety, depersonalization, or motor tics — and they have a brain-computer interface. Is this a psychiatric condition, a device malfunction, or an attack? Today, there is no clinical framework to help you differentiate.

This map traces every documented pathway from BCI exploitation to a DSM-5-TR diagnosis. Each technique is linked to the neural structures it disrupts, the functional systems it impairs, and the clinical presentation that results. The confidence level indicates whether the pathway is established in neuroscience literature, probable based on mechanism, or theoretical.

This is not diagnostic guidance. It is a threat modeling reference that helps clinicians understand what is technically possible when a neural device is compromised — so that emerging symptom presentations can be evaluated in context. The brain's complexity means these projections are necessarily approximate; they map known pathways, not proven outcomes.

Diagnostic Clusters

Attack techniques are grouped into four diagnostic clusters based on their primary psychiatric outcome pattern. Each cluster represents a family of related symptoms that emerge from similar neural disruption mechanisms.

42

Non-Diagnostic

21

Mood/Trauma

17

Cognitive/Psychotic

16

Motor/Neurocognitive

7

Persistent/Personality

Mood / Trauma 21 techniques

Attacks that disrupt limbic circuitry — primarily amygdala, hippocampus, and prefrontal connectivity. Present as depression, anxiety, PTSD-like symptoms, adjustment disorders. Most common cluster because emotional regulation pathways are the most accessible via neurostimulation.

Cognitive / Psychotic 17 techniques

Attacks targeting cortical processing, thalamocortical loops, and dopaminergic pathways. Can produce perceptual distortions, hallucinations, thought disorganization, and cognitive impairment that mimics schizophrenia spectrum, ADHD, or neurocognitive disorders.

Motor / Neurocognitive 16 techniques

Attacks disrupting motor cortex, basal ganglia, cerebellum, or spinal cord pathways. Manifest as involuntary movements, coordination deficits, conversion-like symptoms, or progressive neurocognitive decline. Often the most clinically visible outcomes.

Persistent / Personality 7 techniques

Attacks causing sustained alterations to personality, identity, or behavioral regulation through prolonged or repeated neural manipulation. The most concerning cluster for neurorights — these changes can persist after the attack ceases.

Risk Classification

52

Direct Risk

The attack mechanism directly causes the psychiatric outcome through a documented neural pathway.

9

Indirect Risk

The psychiatric outcome arises secondary to the primary neurological disruption.

Evidence Confidence

Each technique-to-diagnosis link is classified by the strength of the underlying neuroscience evidence connecting the attack mechanism to the clinical outcome.

established (399)
probable (16)
Established (399) — published neuroscience evidence Probable (16) — strong mechanistic basis

Diagnosis Explorer

29 DSM-5-TR diagnoses linked to 61 attack techniques. Expand any diagnosis to see the techniques that can produce it, with their neural pathways and NISS severity scores.

Cognitive/Psychotic NISS 6.4 direct

The Neural Impact Chain

Every technique-to-diagnosis mapping follows a six-stage chain. Understanding this chain helps clinicians trace a presenting symptom back to a potential technical cause.

1

Attack Technique

The specific BCI exploitation method

2

Hourglass Band

Which layer of the neurotechnology stack is targeted

3

Neural Structure

The brain region or pathway disrupted

4

Functional System

The cognitive, emotional, or motor system impaired

5

NISS Score

Severity of the neural impact (0–10 scale)

6

DSM-5-TR Diagnosis

The resulting clinical presentation

See the Full Picture

This page shows the psychiatric outcomes. To trace how each diagnosis arises — from the attack mechanism, through the affected neural band, to the NISS severity score that predicts it — open the full technique entry in the TARA Atlas.

Every technique listed above links directly to its TARA page, where you can see all four projections: the security assessment, therapeutic analog, diagnostic mapping, and governance requirements. The TARA Atlas is the single source of truth — this page is one lens into it.

Open TARA Atlas View all 109 techniques with full scoring and projections

This work needs your expertise.

These mappings were built by a security researcher, not a clinician. They need validation by psychiatrists, clinical neuroscientists, and neuroethicists before they can responsibly inform clinical practice.

If you are a clinician or researcher working in neuroscience, psychiatry, or BCI safety — we welcome peer review, corrections, and collaboration. This map is a starting point built for scrutiny, not prescription.

AI Transparency: This project was developed with AI assistance (Claude, Gemini, ChatGPT). All architectural decisions, threat mappings, and scoring are the author's. AI contributions are documented in the Transparency Statement, Derivation Log, and Validation Status. QIF is a proposed, unvalidated framework. All results are simulation-only unless otherwise noted.
